data.title: Lumine Group (TSXV:LMN) Shift To TTM Profitability Tests Bullish Narrative
data.description:
Lumine Group (TSXV:LMN) closed out FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$216.3 million and basic EPS of US$0.19, while trailing twelve month figures stood at US$765.7 million in revenue and EPS of US$0.46 as earnings moved into positive territory over the past year. The company reported quarterly revenue rising from US$187.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$216.3 million in Q4 2025, with basic EPS increasing from US$0.11 to US$0.19 over the same period, indicating firmer margins underpinning the latest results.

See our full analysis for Lumine Group.

With the headline numbers in place, the next step is to set these results against the prevailing stories about Lumine Group and assess where the data supports the narrative and where it starts to challenge it.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM net income swings from loss to US$118.8 million

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, net income moved from a loss of US$258.9 million in Q4 2024 to a profit of US$118.8 million in Q4 2025, with TTM basic EPS at US$0.46 over the same period.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is how the positive TTM earnings of US$118.8 million line up with quarterly net income of US$49.6 million in Q4 2025, which
    • supports the idea that profitability is now backed by several quarters of positive net income, including US$20.8 million, US$23.6 million and US$24.8 million across Q1 to Q3 2025, not just a single strong quarter,
    • yet also contrasts with the earlier TTM loss of US$1.8b in Q3 2024, so investors who take a bullish stance are still watching how durable this new level of profit proves to be over a longer window.

Stronger headline profits are only part of the picture, and many investors will want to see how this earnings shift ties back to the full company story before getting comfortable with it. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Lumine Group.

Revenue expansion outpaces market forecasts at 16.8%

  • Revenue growth is forecast at 16.8% per year compared with 6% per year for the broader Canadian market, and the latest TTM revenue sits at US$765.7 million versus US$668.4 million a year earlier.
  • Supporters of a bullish angle point to this faster revenue profile as a central part of the thesis, and the numbers give that view some clear backing because
    • quarterly revenue has stayed between US$178.7 million and US$216.3 million across 2025, which lines up with the idea of a larger revenue base feeding into the TTM figure of US$765.7 million,
    • while the move from a TTM net loss of US$258.9 million in Q4 2024 to a TTM profit of US$118.8 million suggests that higher revenue is now associated with earnings that are no longer in loss making territory.

Mixed valuation signals with 41.2x P/E and DCF fair value above price

  • Lumine Group is trading on a trailing P/E of 41.2x, which is lower than its peer average of 56.2x but higher than the North American Software industry at 28.2x, and its CA$26.05 share price is about 29.7% below a CA$37.04 DCF fair value estimate.
  • For more cautious investors, this combination of multiples and fair value estimates creates a clear tension, because
    • critics highlight that paying 41.2x earnings is above the broader software industry’s 28.2x, even though earnings growth of 8.48% per year is below the Canadian market forecast of 11.5% per year,
    • yet supporters may counter that the discount to the CA$37.04 DCF fair value and the shift from a TTM loss to US$118.8 million of TTM net income present a different angle than a simple P/E comparison on its own.

Bayer FY 2025 earnings snapshot

Bayer (XTRA:BAYN) has capped FY 2025 with a tough final quarter, posting Q4 revenue of €11.4 billion and a basic EPS loss of €3.80, alongside a net loss of €3.8 billion. Over recent periods, the company has seen quarterly revenue move between €9.7 billion and €13.7 billion, while EPS has swung from a profit of €1.32 in Q1 2025 to losses in each of the following quarters. This keeps the focus firmly on how quickly margins can stabilize. With forecasts in the data pointing to strong earnings growth and a path back to profitability over the coming years, this set of results puts profitability and cost discipline at the center of the story for investors watching Bayer’s margins.

Losses widen to €3.8b in Q4

  • In Q4 2025, Bayer recorded a net loss of €3.8b and basic EPS of €3.80 loss, compared with a trailing 12 month loss of €3.6b and EPS of €3.68 loss, so the single quarter was slightly worse than the overall year.
  • Bears focus on this pattern, arguing that sizeable quarterly losses together with ongoing litigation and debt concerns keep pressure on cash flow and margins, even as forecasts point to earnings turning positive within three years.
    • The trailing 12 month figures show the company is still loss making, which matches the bearish view that legal and financing costs can weigh on reported profit even when the core business is working to improve.
    • At the same time, the move from a €963 million loss in Q3 to a €3.8b loss in Q4 gives critics more recent data to point to when they argue that earnings volatility has not yet settled.

Revenue holding around €45b a year

  • On a trailing 12 month basis, revenue sits at €45.6b, with quarterly sales moving in a band between €9.7b and €13.7b across the last six reported quarters, so top line has been relatively steady even while profits have been negative.
  • Bulls argue that this revenue base, combined with new pharmaceuticals and agricultural products, can support a swing from a €3.6b loss today to meaningful profits over the next few years if margins improve as forecast.
    • The bullish narrative leans on forecast earnings growth of about 54.8% per year and an expected move back to profitability within three years, which would be a big change from current losses.

DCF fair value far above €37.78 share price

    • Supporters see the low P/S and wide gap to DCF fair value as signs that the market price does not fully reflect the projected earnings path back to profit.
    • Skeptics counter that as long as trailing 12 month net income remains a €3.6b loss and revenue is only expected to rise 2.5% a year against a 6% market rate, the discount may reflect those weaker fundamentals rather than a clear mispricing.

data.title: PagSeguro Digital (NYSE:PAGS) Margin Decline Reinforces Bearish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
PagSeguro Digital (NYSE:PAGS) has just wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of R$5.2b and basic EPS of R$1.74, alongside net income of R$502.0m. These figures put fresh numbers on the table for investors watching its profitability profile. Over recent periods, the company has seen revenue move from R$5.0b in Q4 2024 to R$5.2b in Q4 2025, while basic EPS shifted from R$1.93 to R$1.74. Trailing twelve month EPS stands at R$7.18 on revenue of R$19.7b and net income of R$2.1b, setting the context for how its margins are tracking through this latest print.

Advertisement

Margins Ease Back From 11.5% To 10.7%

  • Over the last 12 months, PagSeguro earned R$2.1b on R$19.7b of revenue, which works out to a 10.7% net margin compared with 11.5% a year earlier.
  • Bears argue that pressure from competition, Pix adoption and higher rates could keep squeezing profitability, and the recent move from 11.5% to 10.7% margin gives them support. However, trailing earnings of R$2.1b still line up with the more cautious view that margins ease rather than collapse.
    • The bearish narrative talks about margin compression from pricing pressure and regulation, and the 0.8 percentage point margin change over the year is consistent with that concern.
    • At the same time, trailing earnings growth over five years of 14.3% a year shows the business has generated profit growth historically even with that margin step down.
Skeptics point to that 10.7% margin and slower recent growth as a warning sign. Yet they also track how the bear case could unfold in more detail in the 🐻 PagSeguro Digital Bear Case.

Five Year EPS Growth Versus Flat Recent Momentum

  • Over five years, earnings have grown about 14.3% a year, while one year earnings growth is around 0.09%, so the long run trend looks strong next to the recent flattening.
  • Bulls focus on that 14.3% yearly earnings growth and argue that business initiatives can sustain EPS progress, but the latest trailing EPS of R$7.18 and near flat 0.09% one year earnings growth mean the current data only partly backs that optimism.
    • Bullish commentary highlights potential for higher profit margins, yet the most recent margin is 10.7% compared with 11.5% previously, so the current margin trend does not yet reflect that view.
    • However, the fact that EPS over the last year sits at R$7.18 and analysts forecast about 9.7% yearly earnings growth suggests bulls are working from both the multi year record and the forecast path rather than just the latest 12 months.
Bulls point to that multi year EPS growth and a R$7.18 trailing EPS base as a starting point, and you can see how they build their case in the 🐂 PagSeguro Digital Bull Case.

P/E Of 7x And DCF Fair Value Gap

  • With the share price at US$10.09 and a trailing P/E of 7x compared with peers at 21.1x and the industry at 18.6x, plus a DCF fair value of US$21.84, the stock screens as much cheaper than those reference points.
  • Consensus commentary flags this valuation gap as a potential opportunity, yet the same data set also shows trailing net margin easing to 10.7% and forecast revenue growth of about 5.3% a year. This helps explain why some investors might question how quickly that 7x P/E could rerate.
    • The single allowed analyst price target of US$12.48 sits above the current US$10.09 share price, so the market price is below both that target and the indicated DCF fair value.
    • On the other hand, the slower forecast revenue growth compared with the cited US market rate, together with margin compression from 11.5% to 10.7%, gives a clear reason why the discount exists in the first place.

data.title: Is EPR Properties (EPR) Pricing Reflect Its Strong Multi‑Year Share Price Performance?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether EPR Properties is offering good value at its current price, this article will walk you through what the numbers are really saying about the stock.
  • The shares last closed at US$57.56, with returns of 4.0% over 30 days, 13.4% year to date, 16.4% over 1 year, 83.3% over 3 years, and 67.3% over 5 years. These figures may influence how investors think about both its potential and its risks.
  • Recent attention on EPR Properties has centered on its positioning as a real estate investment trust focused on experiential properties and how that focus fits investor preferences for income and real asset exposure. This backdrop helps explain why the stock's performance over the past few years is on many investors' radar.
  • On our valuation checklist, EPR Properties scores a 5 out of 6. This sets up a useful comparison between methods like discounted cash flow, multiples, and asset based metrics, with an even more rounded way of thinking about valuation coming at the end of this article.

EPR Properties delivered 16.4% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Specialized REITs industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: EPR Properties Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A DCF model takes the cash that a business is expected to generate in the future, then discounts those cash flows back to today to estimate what the entire company could be worth. For EPR Properties, this is based on adjusted funds from operations, which is commonly used for REITs.

On this approach, EPR Properties is currently generating last twelve months free cash flow of about $398.2 million. Analysts and internal estimates project this to rise to $515.1 million by 2030, with a set of annual cash flow projections out to 2035 that are discounted back to today.

When you add up all those discounted cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $117.71 per share. Against the recent share price of $57.56, this implies the stock is trading at roughly a 51.1% discount to that DCF estimate. This highlights a wide gap between the cash flow based valuation and the current market price.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests EPR Properties is undervalued by 51.1%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for EPR Properties.

Approach 2: EPR Properties Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the bottom line, which makes it a useful cross check against a cash flow model.

What counts as a “normal” P/E really depends on two things: growth expectations and risk. Higher expected earnings growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually means a lower one is more reasonable.

EPR Properties currently trades on a P/E of 17.56x. That sits close to the Specialized REITs industry average of about 16.09x and below the peer group average of 25.68x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for EPR Properties is 33.20x, which is its view of what the P/E could be given the company’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it blends those context factors rather than looking at one dimension. Since the current 17.56x P/E is well below the 33.20x Fair Ratio, the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED
